network-chaos: Introduce packet loss on the node's egress traffic using tc.
Simulate network degradation on the host machine using Netem.
Add 50ms latency to the node's network traffic.
Throttle the uplink bandwidth on the entire worker node.
Make the host's network connection drop packets randomly.
Test how the cluster handles high jitter and slow network links.
Make the whole worker node laggy.
Simulate a saturated uplink on the EC2 instance.
What happens if the underlying host starts dropping packets?
Degrade the physical interface to test cluster timeouts.
Choke the bandwidth on the master node.
Simulate a bad switch port degrading host traffic.
pod-network-chaos: Block incoming traffic on specific ingress ports for the pod.
Prevent the pod from making external connections by blocking egress ports.
Simulate a port-level network isolation for the microservice.
Close off port 8080 on the frontend pod to test timeouts.
Stop the database pod from accepting new connections on its main port.
Prevent the microservice from talking to the outside world.
Close all incoming connections to the web app.
Test what happens when the DB refuses connections.
Make the pod deaf and mute on the network.
Simulate a firewall rule blocking the frontend.
pod-network-filter: Deploy a privileged workload to set iptables rules on the pod.
Filter outgoing UDP protocols on the pod's network interface.
Drop incoming TCP connections on the pod using iptables.
Block traffic on eth0 for the specific container network.
Apply strict protocol-level network filters to isolate the workload.
Drop all UDP packets trying to reach the pod.
Deploy a privileged daemon to mess with the container's iptables.
Simulate a strict security group at the container level.
Blackhole the specific eth0 interface of the pod.
Reject TCP handshakes to the backend service.
Simulate a severed network cable for the specific pod.
Test split-brain scenario by cutting off a pod from the cluster.
node-network-filter: Block outgoing traffic from the worker node's eth0 interface.
Drop incoming traffic on port 2379 across all master nodes using iptables.
Sever node connectivity by filtering all UDP protocols at the host level.
Airgap the entire worker node by manipulating iptables.
Simulate a top-of-rack switch failure by blocking all node interfaces.
Sever the kubelet's connection to the control plane using iptables.
Make the worker node completely unreachable over the network.
Quarantine the compromised machine from the rest of the datacenter.
Drop everything at the host's NIC.
application-outages: Make the application routes inaccessible for 10 minutes.
Block external user traffic from reaching the frontend service.
Simulate a complete application-level network outage.
Disrupt user experience by taking down the OpenShift route.
Users are getting a 503 Service Unavailable from the frontend.
Simulate a DNS routing failure for the external app URL.
Take the website offline for external customers.
Break the ingress controller path to the service.
Simulate a totally broken public URL.
Ensure users get a gateway timeout when hitting the app.
Test the global load balancer failover by killing the route.
service-hijacking: Reroute traffic to a mock krkn web service.
Hijack the service to return a custom 500 HTTP error.
Intercept service calls and reply with a custom MIME type and payload.
Redirect targeted service traffic to a controlled dummy endpoint.
Make the payment API suddenly return a 404.
Spoof the authentication service to test client error handling.
Simulate a compromised microservice returning malicious payloads.
Reroute internal traffic to a sinkhole.
Force the backend to serve the wrong MIME type.
syn-flood: Flood the target service with TCP SYN packets.
Simulate a DDoS attack using a SYN flood.
Overwhelm the external endpoint with a surge of SYN requests.
Exhaust the server's connection tracking table with a high packet size.
Launch a volumetric attack against the load balancer.
Exhaust the connection pool of the web server.
Simulate a botnet hitting the login page.
Spam the target with half-open TCP connections.
Test the WAF's resilience against layer 4 attacks.
pvc-scenarios: Consume 90 percent of the persistent volume claim.
Simulate a full disk on the database PVC.
Fill up the storage volume mounted to the application namespace.
Run a disk fill command on a specific PVC using dd or fallocate.
Trigger a NoSpaceLeftOnDevice error on the database mount.
Test if log rotation failure takes down the application.
Simulate runaway application logging eating the disk.
What happens when the EBS volume hits 100% capacity?
Trigger a disk quota exceeded alert.
Write garbage data to the mount point until it crashes.
Make the disk scream by filling it to the brim.
See what happens when the app runs out of storage space.
pod-scenarios: Delete the frontend pod to test ReplicaSet recreation.
Remove the target pod and wait for it to recover.
Evict the pod from the node.
Simulate a random pod crash in the namespace.
Kill the pod entirely to see if the deployment controller spawns a new one.
Randomly assassinate pods in the namespace.
Test if the deployment heals when pods disappear.
Simulate the OOM killer reaping the whole pod.
Nuke the running instance of the application.
Evict the workload to force a reschedule.
container-scenarios: Send a kill signal 9 to the nginx container specifically.
Hang up the sidecar container using signal 1.
Crash the application container while leaving the parent pod running.
Disrupt a specific container process without terminating the pod.
Simulate a container crash loop by sending recurring kill signals.
Send a segfault signal to the Java process.
Kill just the Istio proxy without touching the main app.
Test liveness probes by freezing the main thread.
Simulate a panic in the application binary.
Force the container runtime to restart just one piece of the pod.
Trigger an exit code 137 inside the workload.
What happens if the main process segfaults?
power-outages: Shut down the entire Kubernetes cluster to simulate a power loss.
Pull the plug on the whole cluster and test recovery.
Forcefully shut down all nodes like a massive AWS blackout.
Simulate a total datacenter power loss.
What happens if the entire AWS region loses power?
Turn off every single machine in the cluster at once.
Test complete disaster recovery from a cold start.
Pull the main breaker on the whole environment.
node-scenarios: Simulate a kernel panic by rebooting the worker node.
Gracefully drain and stop the kubernetes node.
Restart the kubelet service on the target node.
Simulate a cloud provider instance failure.
Test node auto-repair by stopping and starting the VM.
Simulate AWS terminating an EC2 instance.
Test cluster rebalancing when a worker dies.
Reboot the machine out from under the workloads.
What happens if the underlying hypervisor restarts?
Trigger a reboot storm in the cluster.
node-scenarios-bm: Power off the bare metal node via IPMI to simulate a hardware failure.
Trigger a hard reset on the physical server via BMC.
Execute an IPMI node outage command.
Cut power to the baremetal worker node.
Send an iLO or iDRAC kill command to the server.
Simulate someone tripping over the power cord in the server room.
Send a chassis power cycle command.
Test what happens if the physical motherboard fries.
Perform a hard reset via the out-of-band management interface.
Kill the power to the bare metal box.
Simulate a PSU power supply unit failure on the rack.
time-scenarios: Skew the system clock on the target node.
Simulate an NTP sync failure by shifting the date.
Trigger a time drift on the pods in the development namespace.
Move the system time forward to test certificate expiration handling.
Check how the database handles clock drift.
Make the pod think it's living in the year 2030.
Test how the application handles a leap second.
Simulate a severe desync with the time server.
Force the node's clock backward to test token validation.
What if the timestamp suddenly jumps 5 minutes?
Make the server think it is tomorrow.
service-disruption-scenarios: Delete the entire namespace to test cluster recovery.
Wipe out all objects within the target namespace.
Simulate a complete namespace deletion event.
Remove the project and see if the operator recreates it.
Simulate a rogue script deleting the entire environment.
Test the backup operator by destroying the whole tenant.
Wipe the slate clean for the staging project.
Drop the logical partition containing all our microservices.
Accidentally run kubectl delete ns in production.
zone-outages: Take down an entire AWS availability zone.
Block all cross-zone traffic to simulate a VPC outage.
Simulate a public cloud zone failure by dropping subnets.
Restrict VPC ingress and egress to simulate a datacenter failure.
Trigger a regional failover test by blocking the VPC ID.
Cut off routing to an entire subnet.
Test multi-AZ failover by dropping the network link to one zone.
Simulate a localized cloud provider outage.
us-east-1 went down again, simulate that.
kubevirt-outage: Delete the VMI to test automatic recreation.
Simulate a virtual machine failure in OpenShift CNV.
Forcefully terminate the KubeVirt VM instance.
Yank the virtual power cord on the KVM guest.
Simulate a hypervisor fault taking down the VM.
Test if the VirtualMachine runStrategy actually works.
Kill the Windows VM running inside OpenShift.
Pull the plug on the guest OS.
node-cpu-hog: Exhaust CPU resources on the worker node.
Spike the CPU utilization to 100 percent on the target host.
Run a stress-ng CPU hog scenario.
Create severe CPU starvation for all pods on this machine.
Simulate a runaway infinite loop on the host.
Peg all CPU cores at 100 percent.
Test pod throttling under extreme processor contention.
Make the worker node completely unresponsive to SSH due to CPU load.
node-memory-hog: Consume all available RAM on the node.
Trigger an OOM condition by hogging node memory.
Max out memory usage on the host server.
Force the OOM killer to start evicting pods.
Simulate a massive memory leak in the kernel.
Force the system into heavy swap usage.
Eat up all the page cache and free memory.
Push the machine into an out-of-memory death spiral.
Test how the cluster reacts to severe memory pressure.
node-io-hog: Create high disk IO pressure on the node.
Simulate heavy read and write disk operations on the host.
Stress the node filesystem with continuous IO operations.
Thrash the underlying storage disk with a large IO block size.
Simulate a noisy neighbor thrashing the local SSD.
Max out the IOPS on the cloud volume.
Create a massive backlog of disk writes.
Test application latency when the disk queue is full.
Pound the filesystem with heavy random reads.
Simulate a noisy neighbor writing massive log files.
